title: An Anthology of Chancery English: electronic encodings, 1984 edition, and 241 archival sources
summary: ChancEng is a U-M XML selection of 241 documentary texts from Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher's 1984 anthology, transmitted through OTA records 1284 and 1676. It is not one medieval work or manuscript. The edition cites 231 TNA-held source documents/passages and ten passages in seven BL Cotton codices. This manifest preserves those item-level mappings, distinguishes exact current catalog matches from parent-level resolutions, records known editorial errors, caches the public OTA objects and two complete BL manuscript facsimiles, and keeps medieval source-content rights separate from modern edition, encoding, scan, and provider-copy terms.
review_notes: The 241 edition-to-witness relations record source claims, not blanket verification of transcription fidelity; only BL items 14 and 70 were visually matched directly in this review.
TNA audit coverage is complete for all 231 TNA-cited items: 86 exact current-reference matches, 145 parent/ancestor matches, 31 records flagged digitized, and 200 without a public digitized copy.

The 231 TNA mappings resolve to 120 unique current catalog records because many legacy references reach shared parent pieces; repeated current IDs must not be mistaken for proof that the selected source documents are identical.

Complete facsimile bundles mean every canvas exposed by the reviewed provider manifest was cached, including binding and blanks where exposed; they do not claim preservation-master resolution or broaden provider reuse terms.

The Benskin cache is complete for the publisher preview but partial for the full chapter; both the official Pérez Quintero article PDF and a byte-distinct Dialnet mirror are cached completely and preserved without PDF repair.

Known high-value corrections include item 1's date/office, items 106, 107, 148, 150, and 156's Privy Seal classification, item 164's date and Essex locality, item 178's reference and place interpretation, item 234's date, item 238's print-versus-electronic heading conflict, item 240's date, and item 241's 1436 date and deed-poll form.

OTA 1284 internal text SHA-256: anthol1 5a4efddfb3f0e9e4379dd27cbe664f0101cf4628838c6dc6d3199cd55d8fbfe4; anthol2 93b947ef7dc212605175c2011190a90fdd1dd6412672e9c0314bf27331f97db3. OTA 1676 text SHA-256: 8c2bf392d1106ed8f9942ab1213b7df0510d2b704960ef971725e801260ccfed.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for ChancEng
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The root repository's Unlicense notice is not evidence that this upstream-derived text is unrestricted; the CME_from_OTA folder carries a text-specific CC BY-NC-SA notice.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item ChancEng
entities.description: Public electronic text encoding printed pp. 84–303 of the 1984 anthology. The header says front/back matter and printed editorial notes were excluded, although short NOTE1 annotations remain in the XML.

date_statements.label: electronic publication

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive record 1676 (COCOA-tagged version)
entities.description: Single-file COCOA-tagged electronic text deposited by J. D. Burnley in 1992 and later revised. It is the OTA source identifier named by U-M.

date_statements.label: deposit

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive record 1284 (two-file plain-text version)
entities.description: Two-file plain-text electronic text deposited by J. D. Burnley in 1991; its official record points to 1676 as the later COCOA version.

date_statements.label: deposit

entities.label: Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher, An Anthology of Chancery English (1984)
entities.description: A scholarly anthology containing 241 transcribed documentary texts plus introduction, apparatus, bibliography, glossary/index material, and plates. ChancEng encodes only the selected texts on pp. 84–303.

date_statements.label: publication

bibliographic.citation: John H. Fisher, Malcolm Richardson, and Jane L. Fisher, eds., An Anthology of Chancery English (Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1984), xvii, 416 pp., 6 plates
bibliographic.title: An Anthology of Chancery English

physical_description.extent: xvii, 416 pages and 6 pages of plates
physical_description.format: Scholarly monograph and documentary text edition
notes: Includes bibliographical references on pp. 52–62 and an index.
Benskin's critique and his report of Matheson's findings show that the transcription and apparatus must not be treated as diplomatic or error-free without witness checking.

entities.label: Internet Archive controlled scan of the 1984 anthology

physical_description.format: Complete-volume scan
notes: The metadata reports 450 images; access is controlled rather than an unrestricted direct download.

entities.reproduction_format: Page images and controlled PDF/OCR derivatives

entities.label: Pérez Quintero's scholarly description of the Chancery corpus
entities.description: A scholarly linguistic study that describes the anthology's four-part organization and evaluates the corpus for research.

date_statements.label: journal issue date
official OJS online publication date

bibliographic.citation: María Jesús Pérez Quintero, ‘The Adjectival Group in the English Chancery Documents,’ Cuadernos de Investigación Filológica 26 (2000): 137–161
bibliographic.title: The Adjectival Group in the English Chancery Documents

entities.label: Benskin's critique, ‘Chancery Standard’
entities.description: A scholarly critique of the ‘Chancery Standard’ construct and of the 1984 anthology's accuracy and classification. The cached publisher preview covers chapter pp. 1–17, not the whole chapter.

bibliographic.citation: Michael Benskin, ‘Chancery Standard,’ in New Perspectives on English Historical Linguistics II: Lexis and Transmission (John Benjamins, 2004), 1–40
bibliographic.title: Chancery Standard
bibliographic.container_title: New Perspectives on English Historical Linguistics II: Lexis and Transmission

entities.label: ChancEng–TNA Discovery source crosswalk

The commons complain that many people arrested for treason, felony, Lollardy and such crimes, are sent at the King's command to the Tower of London and other castles, where they remain a long time without any process against them; which is very expensive, dangerous to their keepers, and likely to encourage other malefactors. subject.description: Anthology item 1: Signet of Henry V (exemplar)

object.description: Document or passage cited as C81/1326/36

assertion.notes: The relation records the edition's source claim; it does not certify a diplomatic or error-free transcription. Current public catalog resolution reaches only a parent or ancestor record. Benskin shows that this misfiled document is probably a mid-fifteenth-century Privy Seal document associated with John Hamond, not an early Henry V Signet exemplar; the current parent-piece date 1482–1483 must not be assigned to the letter itself.

object.description: Document or passage cited as C146/C1223

assertion.notes: The relation records the edition's source claim; it does not certify a diplomatic or error-free transcription. Benskin corrects the edition's conversion of 24 November, 4 Henry VI from 1426 to 1425; the current catalog retains the regnal wording and a broad [1425–1426] range.

subject.description: Anthology item 237: London Indenture

object.description: Document or passage cited as C146/C3584

assertion.notes: The relation records the edition's source claim; it does not certify a diplomatic or error-free transcription. Benskin corrects the legacy C146/C3584 form to C 146/3584 and cautions that the edition's ‘London’ label does not establish place of production.

subject.description: Anthology item 238: Cambridgeshire Indenture

object.description: Document or passage cited as E101/504/19

assertion.notes: The relation records the edition's source claim; it does not certify a diplomatic or error-free transcription. The XML/U-M heading says ‘Cambridgeshire Indenture’, while Benskin reports the printed anthology heading as ‘Lincoln indenture’. The agreement is dated at and concerns Stamford in Lincolnshire; the print-versus-electronic heading divergence still requires inspection of the controlled print scan.

subject.description: Anthology item 239: London Indenture

object.description: Document or passage cited as E40/A2495

assertion.notes: The relation records the edition's source claim; it does not certify a diplomatic or error-free transcription. The legacy E40/A2495 form is normalized in the current catalog to E 40/2495.

subject.description: Anthology item 240: Northampton Indenture

object.description: Document or passage cited as E40/A7651

assertion.notes: The relation records the edition's source claim; it does not certify a diplomatic or error-free transcription. The current catalog dates E 40/7651 to 4 December 1455, while the edition labels it 1456; ‘Northampton’ describes property and does not prove place of production.

subject.description: Anthology item 241: Devonshire Indenture

object.description: Document or passage cited as C146/C273

assertion.notes: The relation records the edition's source claim; it does not certify a diplomatic or error-free transcription. The legacy C146/C273 form is normalized to C 146/273. Benskin's manuscript collation reads ‘fourteth’ (fourteenth) and dates the record to 1 May 1436, refuting the edition/XML heading's 1462. The current catalog gives only [1400–1500] and repeats the disputed ‘40 Henry VI’ wording in its description. The physical object is cataloged as a deed poll although the document calls itself an indenture.

access.access_method: Direct official journal PDF

local_copies.label: Complete official Pérez Quintero article PDF
notes: The 25-page provider PDF uses RC4 encryption; its permission flags allow printing but restrict copying/extraction and modification. qpdf checks the file successfully while reporting shared-object and hint-table warnings; Poppler read all 25 pages. The downloaded bytes are preserved without repair.

local_copies.label: Complete Pérez Quintero article PDF, Dialnet mirror
notes: This Dialnet copy is textually near-identical but byte-distinct from the official journal PDF. It also uses RC4 encryption; qpdf checks it successfully with shared-object and hint-table warnings, and Poppler read all 25 pages. The downloaded bytes are preserved without repair.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A much larger body of late-medieval governmental and legal records
editorial_practices.representation: 241 documents arranged into four editorially defined groups
editorial_practices.description: The anthology selects records for linguistic and documentary study; it is neither a repository inventory nor a single manuscript edition.
notes: Benskin shows that some source, office, locality, and date labels need correction.

editorial_practices.representation: The title/source metadata and document texts on printed pp. 84–303
editorial_practices.description: The electronic encoding excludes front and back matter and says printed editorial notes were excluded; it is therefore not a complete electronic reproduction of the 1984 book.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML, ChancEng.xml
evidence.summary: The XML header identifies U-M, OTA U-1676-B, and the 1984 edition; its 241 headings preserve item dates, archival references, and edition labels.

evidence.citation: Offline structural and content inspection of ChancEng.xml
evidence.summary: Inspection counted 241 flat DIV1 items and mapped every heading to its cited TNA or BL source reference. It also identified page-milestone anomalies and spot-checked the first and last items.

evidence.citation: CME/source/CME_from_OTA/read_me_license.txt
evidence.summary: The source-folder notice states that texts derived from OTA are licensed CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; this text-specific notice is distinct from the parent repository's Unlicense software notice.

evidence.citation: General Corpus README rights statement
evidence.summary: The project describes its own output licensing and commercial goals, which cannot enlarge rights granted by upstream text, edition, or image providers.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, An anthology of Chancery English (ChancEng)
evidence.summary: The CME item supplies the public digital text, source-edition description, OTA identifier, and U-M rights/access statements.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, An anthology of Chancery English, record 1284
evidence.summary: Official record for J. D. Burnley's 1991 two-file plain-text deposit, revised in 1998 and 2002, licensed CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; it points to a later COCOA version at record 1676.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, An anthology of Chancery English, record 1676
evidence.summary: Official record for J. D. Burnley's 1992 single-file COCOA deposit, revised in 1997, 2002, and 2016, licensed CC BY-NC-SA 3.0. Its note contains a self-link/display-number defect, but the reciprocal 1284 record supports the version sequence.

evidence.citation: John H. Fisher, Malcolm Richardson, and Jane L. Fisher, eds., An Anthology of Chancery English (Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1984), xvii + 416 pp., 6 plates
evidence.summary: Catalog and encoded source statements establish the editors, title, publication, extent, bibliography, index, ISBN, LCCN, and OCLC identity of the source edition.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive controlled scan, anthologyofchanc0000unse
evidence.summary: A 450-image complete-volume scan is available through controlled digital lending; PDF/OCR derivatives are private or encrypted and were not cached.

evidence.citation: María Jesús Pérez Quintero, ‘The Adjectival Group in the English Chancery Documents,’ Cuadernos de Investigación Filológica 26 (2000): 137–161
evidence.summary: Reports 241 texts and the four edition-defined groups and relays the edition's claim of faithful representation; that claim is qualified by later textual criticism. The official item page supplies DOI 10.18172/cif.2224, an online-publication date of 2013-07-15 for the volume dated 2000, and an explicit CC BY 4.0 article license.

evidence.citation: Michael Benskin, ‘Chancery Standard,’ in New Perspectives on English Historical Linguistics II: Lexis and Transmission, ed. Christian Kay, Carole Hough, and Irené Wotherspoon (John Benjamins, 2004), 1–40
evidence.summary: Critiques the anthology's transcription accuracy, references, dates, localities, and administrative classifications; specifically discusses items 1, 40, 81, 106, 107, 148, 150, 156, 164, 178, 234, and 237–241, and reports Matheson's error count. The preview supports only chapter pp. 1–17, so claims outside that range are not inferred.

evidence.citation: General Corpus ChancEng crosswalk against The National Archives Discovery API
evidence.summary: A reproducible 231-item snapshot preserving literal anthology references, normalized queries, current catalog IDs/references, details responses, exact-versus-parent status, and digitization flags. It found 86 exact matches, 145 parent/ancestor matches, and 31 digitized records.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, Discovery API terms
evidence.summary: Defines permitted use of Discovery API metadata and links the metadata to the Open Government Licence.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, Our fees and free record-download limits
evidence.summary: Explains account, basket, fair-use, download-limit, and charging conditions; automated bulk downloading is not allowed.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, How to order, view and copy documents
evidence.summary: Explains onsite consultation and paid record-copy ordering for records without public digital copies.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, Copyright and reuse terms
evidence.summary: Distinguishes OGL catalog information from record images and copies; publication or commercial use of supplied images may require Image Library permission and fees.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog records for seven Cotton codices cited in ChancEng
evidence.summary: Current official records identify the seven physical codices, shelfmarks, access conditions, contents, and available digitized-content links. Cotton MS Vespasian F XIII/1 has a historical full-coverage note but no working current public manifest was found.

evidence.citation: British Library IIIF manifest for Cotton MS Vespasian C XII
evidence.summary: Complete provider sequence of 707 canvases; item 14's matching English letter is on provider-labeled f. 155r, canvas 325.

evidence.citation: Direct visual inspection of Cotton MS Vespasian C XII, f. 155r, canvas 325
evidence.summary: The English letter beginning ‘Worshipful fader in god’ matches anthology item 14; the current catalog instead describes the John Hull order at f. 154r.

evidence.citation: British Library IIIF manifest for Cotton MS Vespasian F III
evidence.summary: Complete provider sequence of 430 canvases; item 70 is at f. 8r, canvas 25.

evidence.citation: Direct visual inspection of Cotton MS Vespasian F III, f. 8r, canvas 25
evidence.summary: The manuscript text beginning ‘Furthremore I wole’ matches anthology item 70.

evidence.citation: British Library digitisation services
evidence.summary: Explains reference and reproduction image ordering and fees for manuscripts without usable public digital coverage, and directs licensing enquiries to licensing@bl.uk.

evidence.citation: British Library Imaging Services terms and conditions
evidence.summary: The current Imaging Services terms state that images supplied by the British Library may not be copied or reproduced without written permission, that fees may apply, and that permission enquiries should be sent to permissions@bl.uk.

evidence.citation: British Library website terms and image reuse conditions
evidence.summary: Provider terms and item-specific rights remain applicable to digital images; digitized-manuscript metadata states public domain in most countries other than the UK but does not supply a blanket worldwide commercial license.

evidence.citation: United States Copyright Office, Copyright Term and the Public Domain / 17 U.S.C. chapter 3
evidence.summary: A 1984 edition is not public domain merely because its medieval source records are old; post-1977 publication-term rules apply to original editorial matter.

open_questions.question: Which of the anthology's 241 transcriptions and editorial interventions are accurate when collated against the physical records or new facsimiles?
next_steps: Collate each text against a facsimile or repository reproduction.
Record supplied text, expansions, deletions, insertions, lineation, punctuation, and omissions item by item.
Prioritize items identified by Benskin and Matheson as erroneous.

open_questions.question: Can ChancEng-derived text, formatting research, or facsimile images be redistributed through a repository or commercial/POD workflow?
next_steps: Obtain written clarification from U-M/OTA and the 1984 edition rightsholder before commercial reuse of the electronic text or substantial edition-derived material.
Request separate publication permission for TNA or BL images where provider terms require it.
Treat local caches as research evidence, not redistributable assets, unless an item-specific license permits publication.

open_questions.question: Can the 145 TNA references currently resolved only to parent or ancestor records be reconciled to exact modern item-level records?
next_steps: Use printed apparatus and series-specific finding aids to interpret legacy references.
Ask TNA staff to resolve colon-separated, malformed, joined-fragment, and superseded references.
Update each witness entity without deleting the inherited literal reference.

open_questions.question: Can complete or relevant-leaf facsimiles be obtained for Cotton MSS Galba B I, Julius B VI, Vespasian F I, Vespasian F XIII/1, and Cleopatra F IV?
next_steps: Request reference-image quotes from BL digitisation services, preferably for complete codices when feasible.
Ask BL whether the historical full digital coverage for Vespasian F XIII/1 can be restored or supplied.
Record exact folio/canvas mappings and provider reuse terms for any supplied images.

open_questions.question: Why does the XML/U-M version label item 238 ‘Cambridgeshire Indenture’ while Benskin reports the printed anthology heading as ‘Lincoln indenture’ for the Stamford agreement?
next_steps: Inspect the controlled print scan at item 238 and record the exact printed heading.
Determine whether the electronic heading reflects an encoding error, a pre-publication source, or a later editorial change.
Preserve the document's Stamford/Lincolnshire evidence separately from either editorial heading.

open_questions.question: Which foliation should be cited for anthology item 14 in Cotton MS Vespasian C XII: the edition/provider's f. 155r or the current catalog's f. 154r?
next_steps: Ask BL to reconcile the catalog description, physical foliation, and IIIF canvas label.
Continue to cite canvas 325 as the verified digital location while preserving both folio reports.

open_questions.question: Can OTA correct record 1676's internal note, which labels/links the earlier version inconsistently?
next_steps: Report the self-link/display-number defect to OTA.
Retain the version relation based on the reciprocal 1284 record and deposit chronology.

open_questions.question: Are the missing and out-of-order XML page milestones inherited conversion defects, and do they affect generated page-reference navigation?
next_steps: Compare milestone order against the 1984 print scan under controlled access.
Do not infer missing edition pages or manuscript content from milestone anomalies alone.
notes: Absent PB labels: 141, 192, 193, and 293; several labels occur out of numeric order, including 208/215/216 and 270/287/271.
